Computer Vision
entries.summary: Computer vision is about turning images into features that preserve spatial structure.

entries.summary: Data work is about preparing inputs so models learn stable signal instead of leakage, noise, or brittle shortcuts.

entries.title: Data Preprocessing
entries.summary: Preprocessing decides whether downstream models learn signal or shortcuts.

entries.title: Deep Learning
entries.summary: Deep learning is about learning useful functions by composing linear maps, nonlinearities, and gradient-based updates.

entries.title: Deep Learning Training Techniques
entries.summary: Training techniques are the practical layer between a correct model and a stable training run.

entries.title: Diffusion Models
entries.summary: Diffusion models learn to reverse a gradual noising process, which makes training stable but sampling relatively expensive.

entries.summary: Distributed training and serving are mostly about communication patterns and where state lives.

entries.summary: Embeddings turn raw or discrete inputs into dense vectors whose geometry can be searched, compared, and reused.

entries.title: Evaluation
entries.summary: This section is about how to measure whether an agent succeeds, whether its tool calls work, and where failures happen.

entries.title: Generative Models
entries.summary: Generative modeling is about learning a distribution that can produce useful new samples.

entries.title: GPU Performance
entries.summary: GPU reasoning should connect model math to memory traffic and achievable throughput.

entries.summary: Guardrails are the checks around an agent loop that prevent unsafe, malformed, or low-confidence actions.

entries.summary: Use this path if your main goal is ML interview readiness rather than full production depth.

entries.summary: Schedules shape how aggressively optimization explores early and settles late.

entries.summary: Linear models are the baseline family for prediction, calibration, and interpretation.

entries.title: LLM Alignment
entries.summary: Alignment is the stack that turns a capable next-token model into one that follows instructions and preferences.

entries.title: LLM Evaluation
entries.summary: LLM evaluation asks five different questions: - does the model assign high probability to text? - does it produce a correct answer? - does it stay consistent across samples? - does…

entries.title: LLM Inference Serving
entries.summary: Serving quality depends on latency, memory, batching, and hardware efficiency more than on model architecture alone.

entries.title: LLM Long Context
entries.summary: Long-context systems are about fitting more information into the prompt without making inference too slow or too expensive.

entries.title: LLM Reasoning
entries.summary: Reasoning models spend more inference compute to improve performance on harder tasks.

entries.title: LLM Tokenization
entries.summary: Tokenization decides what the model sees as a unit and what the system pays for in context length.

entries.title: Machine Learning
entries.summary: Use this track to learn the ML stack from first principles without getting buried in low-value variants.

entries.title: Math for ML
entries.summary: ML fundamentals are the minimum math ideas that make model behavior legible.

entries.title: Math Foundations for ML
entries.summary: Math connects model behavior to geometry, curvature, and optimization.

entries.title: Memory
entries.summary: This section is about how an agent keeps useful state across steps and turns without replaying the full conversation every time.

entries.title: Metric Learning
entries.summary: Metric learning shapes an embedding space so distance directly reflects similarity or preference.

entries.title: Metrics and Evaluation
entries.summary: Metrics only matter when they match the question you actually care about.

entries.title: ML Monitoring
entries.summary: Monitoring is how you detect that a deployed model is still serving traffic but no longer serving value.

entries.title: ML Serving
entries.summary: Serving is the operational layer between a trained model and a real workload.

entries.title: ML Systems
entries.summary: ML systems are about how training and inference actually run on hardware.

entries.title: MLOps and Production ML
entries.summary: Production ML is mostly about keeping a useful model useful after deployment.

entries.title: Models and Classical ML
entries.summary: Classical ML is still the fastest path to good baselines, interpretable behavior, and small-data wins.

Multi-Agent
entries.summary: This section is about when more than one worker is useful, how roles should differ, and how results should be merged back into one answer.

entries.title: Multimodal LLMs
entries.summary: Modern LLMs often consume more than text, but the core systems question is still how those inputs fit into one sequence budget.

entries.title: NLP and LLMs
entries.summary: This section is about how modern language models represent text, train, decode, align, and serve.

entries.title: Observability
entries.summary: This section is about how to trace an agent run, inspect the steps it took, and measure where failures or latency come from.

entries.title: Optimization and Training Dynamics
entries.summary: Optimization is about turning gradients into stable parameter updates.

entries.title: Overflow Metrics
entries.summary: Overflow metrics describe what happens when examples exceed a hard token or length budget.

entries.title: Planning
entries.summary: Planning is the layer that turns a broad goal into ordered steps the agent can execute and track.

entries.title: Probability for ML
entries.summary: Probability gives models a language for uncertainty, sampling, and latent variables.

entries.title: Prompting
entries.summary: Prompting is the layer that turns a raw user request into a structured input the model can reliably follow.

entries.title: RAG
entries.summary: RAG is the pattern where an agent retrieves outside knowledge and then answers using that retrieved context.

entries.title: Reinforcement Learning
entries.summary: Reinforcement learning is about choosing actions that improve long-run reward, not just immediate reward.

entries.title: Representation Learning
entries.summary: Representation learning asks how to turn raw input into geometry that makes downstream tasks easier.

entries.title: RL for LLMs
entries.summary: RL for LLMs is preference optimization under long sequences, expensive sampling, and fragile reward signals.

entries.title: Statistics for ML
entries.summary: Statistics turns noisy observations into estimates, intervals, and decisions.

entries.title: Transition Indicators and Masks
entries.summary: Transition indicators turn episode termination state into tensors that control bootstrapping and loss masking.

entries.title: Trees and Ensembles
entries.summary: Tree-based models dominate many tabular problems because they capture nonlinear structure with limited preprocessing.

entries.title: Uncertainty
entries.summary: Uncertainty is useful only when it changes a decision, threshold, or deployment policy.

Vision Architectures
entries.summary: CNN architectures mainly differ in how they scale depth, downsampling, and skip connections.

entries.title: Vote and Minority-Cluster Metrics
entries.summary: Vote metrics summarize how multiple sampled answers agree, disagree, or fragment into alternatives.

entries.title: Workflows
entries.summary: This section is about how an agent routes tasks, hands state to another step or worker, and decides when to retry.
